text: <@UE6EFEPTQ> Yeah, should have posted to <#C5T9GPWFL|general> but whatevs. I checked out Term Rewriting And All That from my library today. So far it’s concerned with (equational, i.e. first order) term rewriting as computation: normal forms, confluence, termination. Concepts that seemed important if I wanted to write optimizations for a compiler, say, or to prove things about programs written in a term rewriting system. It wasn’t clear to me that I needed to know much while I prototype and build simple applications.

text: I thought that Lively Web was an old and abandoned project, but that is not true at all. Lots of interesting-looking stuff at <http://lively-next.org>.

attachments.title: lively.next
attachments.text: lively.next is a personal programming kit. It emphasizes liveness, directness and interactivity.

It’s sad that the tovertafel platform is so expensive - and has a pretty narrow vision. I suppose narrowing the vision to “games for people with special needs” makes it easier to deliver commercially on a shorter timescale… rather than trying to educate the world that “apps” isn’t a good way to proceed.

:ghost: Folks, we are releasing <http://Fibery.io|Fibery.io> (#nocode platform to organize work) soon and want to improve getting started experience. If you can *record with voice* your first 30 mins in Fibery and share it with us that would be extremely helpful! Please contact me <mailto:new@fibery.io|new@fibery.io> if you want to participate.

text: Oh cool. The tooling Josh and I are building for ourselves is based on a mixture of StateCharts, dataflow, and queries.
Oh cool. The tooling Josh and I are building for ourselves is based on a mixture of StateCharts, dataflow, and queries.
one of the main things missing from Eve was some sort of state machine-like abstraction
one of the main things missing from Eve was some sort of state machine-like abstraction

the language I designed based on everything we'd learned through Eve was dataflow with state machines embedded in it, but it turns out if you flip that the other way around you get something pretty neat
